agents.name: birdcatcher

events.name: The Birdcatcher Lives
events.description: Near Constantinople there lived a man who knew no other occupation but that of catching birds; his neighbours called him the birdcatcher. Some he used to sell, others served him for food, and thus he maintained himself.
thoughts: The text describes a man's daily life and occupation without any conflict or tension. It seems to be setting up the character and his situation.

events.name: Birdcatcher Decides What to Do
events.description: One day he caught a crow, and wanted to let it go, but then he had nothing to take home. ‘If I can’t catch anything to-day, I’ll take my children the crow, that they may amuse themselves; and they have no other birds at hand.’ So he intended, and so he did.

events.name: Crow Offers Bird Service
events.description: His wife, on seeing the crow, said: ‘What mischief have you brought me? Wring the worthless thing’s neck!’ The crow, on hearing that sentence, besought the birdcatcher to let her go, and promised to be always at his service. ‘I will bring birds to you; through me you will become prosperous.’

events.name: Crow Released by Birdcatcher
events.description: ‘Even if you’re lying, it’s no great loss,’ said the birdcatcher to himself, and set the crow at liberty.

events.name: Birdcatcher Catches Nightingales
events.description: On the morrow the birdcatcher went out birdcatching as usual, and the crow kept her word; she brought him two nightingales; he caught them both, and took them home.
thoughts: The event involves the hero acting within the core plot, as he goes out birdcatching and catches two nightingales with the crow's help. This is a continuation of previous events, where the birdcatcher released the crow on the promise that it would bring him birds.

events.name: Grand Vizier Saves Nightingales
events.description: The nightingales were not long with the birdcatcher, for the grand vizier heard of them, sent for the birdcatcher, took the two nightingales from him, and placed them in the new mosque.

events.name: Summoned by the Emperor
events.description: ‘It’s no joke to go before the emperor! I know why he summons me; no half torture will be mine. I am guilty of nothing, I owe nothing; but the emperor’s will, that’s my crime!’ said the birdcatcher, and went into the emperor’s presence all pale with fear.

events.description: ‘Birdcatcher, sirrah! are you the catcher of those nightingales which were at the new mosque.’ ‘Padishah! both father and mother! where your slipper is, there is my face!–I am.’

events.name: Birdcatcher Summoned by Emperor
events.description: His wife summoned him to supper; just then the crow was at the window: ‘What’s this?’ the crow asked. What are these lamentations? What’s the distress?’ ‘Let me alone; don’t add to my torture; I’m done for owing to you!’ said the birdcatcher, and told her all.

events.name: Birdcatcher Transformed by Fire
events.description: The birdcatcher mounted the emperor’s best horse, galloped around it, dismounted when it sweated, anointed himself with its foam, and then jumped into the fire. He emerged from the flames as a young man of twenty years old.
thoughts: The birdcatcher's transformation into a young man through fire marks a significant plot development and a turning point in his journey. This event resolves the conflict between the birdcatcher and the sultana, who had been seeking revenge against him.
